coredata.dc:title: Interaction mechanism for ionic nitrogen-doped carbon dots and ZDDP combinations as lubricant additives
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Pei, Xiaohu
Liu, Xiaoling
Wang, Haocheng
Chen, Jiali
Guo, Feng
coredata.dc:description: The advent of low-viscosity lubricants has enhanced the economic viability of fuel utilization. As a concomitant effect, machine workpieces are more prone to operate in the context of boundary or mixed lubrication regime with thin oil film, which in turn elevates the probability of damage to parts. Consequently, there is an increased requirement for the tribological properties of the conventional anti-wear additive zinc dialkyl dithiophosphate (ZDDP) to be more robust. In this study, we have selected the combination of a low amount of ionic nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) and ZDDP as an additive, the interaction of combined additive serves to mitigate the high friction characteristics of ZDDP while simultaneously enhancing its low wear performance, offering a highly effective solution for a common industry challenge. The wear surface analyses demonstrate that the combination of NCD cations, phosphate ester anions and ZDDP adsorbed on the tribo-pair forms a phosphate hybrid tribofilm with carbon-rich on the surface through tribochemical reaction, which works a synergistic lubrication effect in the friction process, demonstrating the excellent tribological properties of the combined additive.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZDDP
Ionic nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Interaction mechanism
Additive

The authors gratefully acknowledge the support by the National Natural Science Foundation of People's Republic of China (grants No. 52375190 and No. 52005278) and the \u2018Taishan Scholar\u2019 Talents Project from Shandong province (grants No. TS20190943).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical images of the lubricants employed in the test after 15 days standing: (a) PAO10; (b) PAO10 + 1 % ZDDP; (c) PAO10 + 0.015 % [NCDs][DEHP]; (d) PAO10 + 1 % ZDDP + 0.015 % [NCDs][DEHP].

(a) Curves of friction coefficient versus time of PAO10 and PAO10 with different concentrations [NCDs][DEHP], (b) Average friction coefficient and wear volume of the sliding steel block under different concentrations [NCDs][DEHP]. Load: 70 N, frequency: 3 Hz, amplitude: 4 mm.

Curves of friction coefficient versus time: load 70 N, sliding frequency: (a) 1 Hz, (b) 3 Hz, (c) 5 Hz, and sliding frequency 3 Hz, load: (d) 35 N, (e) 70 N, (f) 140 N. Average friction coefficient and wear volume for various (g) sliding frequencies and (h) loads.

Surface wear morphology of steel blocks after friction experiments and EDS analysis results for (a) PAO10, (b) PAO10 + ZDDP, (c) PAO10 + [NCDs][DEHP], (d) PAO10 + ZDDP + [NCDs][DEHP]. (70 N, 3 Hz, 4 mm).
XPS spectrum surface characteristic elements of worn surfaces on steel blocks lubricated by PAO10 + [NCDs][DEHP] and PAO10 + ZDDP + [NCDs][DEHP]. (70 N, 3 Hz, 4 mm).
Raman spectrum of worn surface under the load of (a) 70 N and (b) 140 N. (3 Hz, 4 mm).
Morphology of tribofilm on worn surface under different additives: (a) ZDDP, (b) [NCDs][DEHP], (c) ZDDP + [NCDs][DEHP]. (d) XPS elemental depth profile.
Lubrication mechanism diagram of combined additives.
Physical properties of PAO10 and PAO10 with various additives.

head.ce:title: Interaction mechanism for ionic nitrogen-doped carbon dots and ZDDP combinations as lubricant additives

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical & Automotive Engineering, Qingdao University of Technology, Qingdao 266520, Shandong, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanical & Automotive Engineering, Qingdao University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical & Automotive Engineering, Qingdao University of Technology, Shandong Qingdao 266520, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, Gansu, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Gansu Lanzhou 730000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The advent of low-viscosity lubricants has enhanced the economic viability of fuel utilization. As a concomitant effect, machine workpieces are more prone to operate in the context of boundary or mixed lubrication regime with thin oil film, which in turn elevates the probability of damage to parts. Consequently, there is an increased requirement for the tribological properties of the conventional anti-wear additive zinc dialkyl dithiophosphate (ZDDP) to be more robust. In this study, we have selected the combination of a low amount of ionic nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) and ZDDP as an additive, the interaction of combined additive serves to mitigate the high friction characteristics of ZDDP while simultaneously enhancing its low wear performance, offering a highly effective solution for a common industry challenge. The wear surface analyses demonstrate that the combination of NCD cations, phosphate ester anions and ZDDP adsorbed on the tribo-pair forms a phosphate hybrid tribofilm with carbon-rich on the surface through tribochemical reaction, which works a synergistic lubrication effect in the friction process, demonstrating the excellent tribological properties of the combined additive.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The use of low-viscosity lubricants in industrial lubrication processes has been the subject of growing interest in recent times [1–4], largely due to their favorable characteristics in terms of low viscous resistance, high fuel economy and significant improvement in engine efficiency. However, it is important to note that low-viscosity lubricants could also have the potential to cause mechanical components to operate for extended periods in thin oil film under mixed lubrication regime [5], which could in turn lead to an increased risk of surface damage.

The certain active additives present in the lubricated condition, especially ZDDP dispersed within the lubricating medium, the contact pressure and shear force generated on the interacting surfaces triggers the tribochemical reaction at the sliding interface [6], resulting in the formation of nanoscale tribofilms that are dynamic growth and removal, which enhances the anti-friction performance of low-viscosity lubricants. Consequently, the solid-like tribofilms formed by ZDDP are employed to avoid direct contact between the two surfaces [7], thereby functioning as a protective barrier. In this case, the Zn content is higher in the upper part of the ZDDP tribofilm, while a significant quantity of Fe can be found in the lower part of the tribofilm. Furthermore, S enrichment is evident between the tribofilm and the metal surface [8]. The investigation about the formation mechanism of ZDDP tribofilm has revealed that tribofilms are formed during sliding contact [9], but sliding is not a requisite for their formation. ZDDP tribofilm can also be generated in the absence of solid-solid rubbing contact, driven by applied shear stresses, which is in accordance with the stress-promoted thermal activation model [10].

Despite the excellent anti-wear properties of ZDDP tribofilm, it has been demonstrated that ZDDP can have negative effects in the mixed lubrication regime. This is due to the rough surface of the tribofilm, which can inhibit the entrainment of lubricant and lead to high friction [11,12] . Furthermore, the presence of high friction results in a reduction in engine fuel economy [13]. Consequently, reducing high friction while maintaining anti-wear properties has become a significant challenge in contemporary engineering. The combined use of additives has emerged as a highly effective approach to mitigate high friction [14–17] .

Inorganic additives of nanomaterials are anticipated to demonstrate excellent performance due to their distinctive self-mending properties [18], polishing effects [19], and robust deposition film-forming capability [20]. Carbon dots (CDs), a type of carbon nanomaterial, possess a multitude of advantageous characteristics, including abundant surface groups, diminutive size, and robust capacity for interfacial adsorption. These attributes render them highly promising as lubrication additives [21,22] . The dispersion of nanoparticles in base oil represents a significant challenge in the context of lubrication application [23,24] . Among these, ionic liquid (IL) modified carbon dots exhibit promising oil solubility [25,26] . Furthermore, the strong adsorption film-forming ability of poly (ionic liquids) brush-grafted carbon dots (CDs-PIL) can provide certain mend to the metal worn interface during the rubbing process [27], and the excellent tribological performance is attributed to the synergistic effect of carbon cores and IL groups. The authors previously proposed a modification method to obtain an ionic structure through nitrogen alkylation reaction and to improve oil compatibility through anion exchange. This method allows the synthesis of NCDs with an ionic structure without the need for pre-preparation of IL, while still providing effective lubrication when the dosage of ionic NCDs is low [28]. The self-mending and robust adsorption capacity of NCDs render them an appropriate choice for the enhancement of lubricant performance. It is therefore of great significance to explore how to combine the characteristics of ionic NCDs with the advantages of traditional additive ZDDP, namely the ability to form a tribofilm in a short time. This will allow for the optimization of the composition of ZDDP and ionic NCDs tribofilm, as well as an understanding of their interaction mechanism.

In this study, a mixed lubricant was formed by combining a low additive amount of ionic nitrogen-doped carbon dots ([NCDs][DEHP]), ZDDP and PAO. The underlying mechanism of tribofilm formation of the combined additives and its synergistic effect regime were explored. By analyzing the composition and structure of the tribofilm with combining the results of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) ion beam depth sputtering analysis, a method is proposed to ameliorate the high friction caused by surface roughening of ZDDP tribofilm by utilizing the self-mending property of [NCDs][DEHP].

The low-viscosity base oil, designated PAO10, was procured from Beijing Xirunte Trading Company. The commercial additive ZDDP-T203 (zinc dioctyl dithiophosphate) was obtained from Jinzhou Shengda Chemical Company Limited. The synthesis process of [NCDs][DEHP] has been described in detail in our previous work [28], as a supplement, the fluorescence property of [NCDs][DEHP] is given in Fig. S1. The molecular structures of ZDDP and [NCDs][DEHP] are presented in Fig. 1. The contents of Zn, P, and S in ZDDP-T203 are 9.7 wt%, 6.9 wt%, and 15.6 wt%, respectively. [NCDs][DEHP] contains 3.54 wt% of P. The physical properties of PAO10 and PAO10 with different additives are listed in Table 1.
It can be observed from Table 1 that both the single additive and the combination of additives have a minimal effect on the viscosity, density viscosity index and pour point of the base oil.

The tribological properties of ZDDP with [NCDs][DEHP] were evaluated utilizing universal mechanical tester (UMT-TriboLab, BRUKER, Germany). In the ball-on-block reciprocating sliding device, the upper part of the tribo-pair is constituted by a stationary steel ball (SAE 52100, mean surface roughness R a1 = 14 nm, diameter 8 mm), while the lower part is constituted by sliding steel block (SAE-52100, mean surface roughness R a2 = 16.9 nm, 20 × 10 × 9 mm). The hardness of ball and block is determined to be approximately 61 ± 1 HRC.

In the course of the testing phase, with reference to the test conditions in Table S1, the applied load was 70 N at room temperature for a duration of 3600 s, the frequency was 3 Hz, and the amplitude was 4 mm. To ensure the validity of the data, three parallel samples were tested in each group.

High precision surface profiler (PGI 800, Taylor Hobson, UK) was employed to measure the wear surface roughness and depth of abrasion marks, thereby enabling the calculation of the wear volume. Confocal laser microscope (VK-X1050, KEYENCE, Japan) was utilized to record the wear surface topography. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, CARL ZEISS, Germany) was used to observe the wear scars at an accelerating voltage of 15 kV and to detect its chemical distribution by energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). The chemical composition of the abrasion surfaces was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (K-Alpha XPS, Thermo Scientific, USA) using Al K-Alpha as the X-ray source. Subsequently, the surface chemical composition was analyzed at varying depths through the utilization of XPS Ar ion sputtering. The Raman analyses were obtained by a laser Raman spectrometer (Raman, Thermo Scientific DXR2, USA) with a controlled laser source of 532 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solubility of additives in base oil PAO10
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in Fig. 2, the addition of ZDDP, [NCDs][DEHP] and ZDDP + [NCDs][DEHP] as additives exhibiting sustained dispersion stability in the base oil PAO10 following a 15-day period of standing at room temperature.

To ascertain the optimal concentration of [NCDs][DEHP], [NCDs][DEHP] with various proportions (mass fraction: 0.005 %, 0.01 %, 0.015 %, 0.02 %, and 0.025 %) were designated for testing. The tribological properties of low effective addition [NCDs][DEHP] as additive in PAO10 were evaluated utilizing the UMT-TriboLab friction and wear tester. As illustrated in Fig. 3(a), the friction coefficient of base oil PAO10, exhibited a stabilized value of approximately 0.105 following the initial period of wear under the specified experimental conditions. The addition of [NCDs][DEHP] resulted in a decrease in the friction coefficient by 6.67 % and 15.24 % compared to PAO10, with the former addition at 0.01 % and the latter at 0.015 %. As illustrated in Fig. 3(b), the addition of 0.015 % [NCDs][DEHP] yielded the lowest average friction coefficient and wear volume, with a 50.87 % reduction in wear volume compared to PAO10. This highlighted the excellent friction reduction and wear resistance performance of the selected concentration. In consideration of the anti-wear and friction reduction properties, 0.015 % was selected for the subsequent investigation of the interfacial interaction mechanism. The optimal concentration of [NCDs][DEHP] also remains at 0.015 % when ZDDP is introduced (see Fig. S2).

ZDDP has been extensively employed as an anti-wear additive. Despite its remarkable anti-wear characteristics, high friction may arise under mixed lubrication regime [7,11,12], which can readily result in a reduction in fuel economy. In light of the aforementioned considerations, we put forth the proposition of employing [NCDs][DEHP] in conjunction with ZDDP as a means of mitigating the adverse effects of high friction observed in ZDDP. Accordingly, an investigation was conducted into the tribological properties of [NCDs][DEHP] in combination with ZDDP as an additive. Furthermore, given that the typical concentration of ZDDP in commercially available oils is 1 wt% [29,30] , the addition of ZDDP was maintained at this concentration in the experiments.

Fig. 4(a-c) illustrate the friction coefficient for a contact load of 70 N and sliding frequencies of 1, 3 and 5 Hz. From Fig. 4(b-c), it can be observed that the friction coefficient of ZDDP is 2.85 % and 7.66 % higher, respectively, in comparison to the PAO10, which exhibit ZDDP’s high friction characteristic. It is noteworthy that ZDDP does not display high friction all the time. As illustrated in Fig. 4(a), ZDDP demonstrates effective friction reduction, exhibiting a friction coefficient that is 18.87 % lower than that of the PAO10. The analysis of diverse lubrication characteristics (high friction and friction reduction) of ZDDP suggests that the ZDDP tribofilm liner structure increases the roughness of the contact surfaces [31], which results in different abilities to limit the lubricant entrainment to the contact zone at high sliding frequency versus low sliding frequency [32]. This is due to the fact that the liner structure is more restrictive at high sliding frequency, which therefore exhibits high friction. In contrast, [NCDs][DEHP] have been observed to exhibit a notable reduction in friction coefficient at high sliding frequency (5 Hz). The combination of [NCDs][DEHP] and ZDDP result in a significantly lower level of wear when compared to the use of ZDDP and [NCDs][DEHP] individually (Fig. 4(g)). Therefore, the high friction characteristic of ZDDP is effectively reduced by the combined use of the additives (see Fig. 4(b) and (c)), which indicates that the combined additives exhibit notable anti-wear and friction reduction performance.

Fig. 4(d-f) illustrate the friction coefficient at a sliding frequency of 3 Hz and contact loads of 35, 70 and 140 N, respectively. It can be observed that the friction coefficient of ZDDP is higher than that of the PAO10 at varying contact loads and continue to demonstrate high friction characteristic. The average friction coefficient and wear volume of [NCDs][DEHP] demonstrate an upward trajectory with increasing load (Fig. 4(h)). It is worthy of note that the average friction coefficient of [NCDs][DEHP] at 140 N is less than that of ZDDP. Nevertheless, the wear volume is 29.61 % higher than that of ZDDP. This indicates that [NCDs][DEHP] does not provide effective lubrication at high load. The combination of [NCDs][DEHP] with ZDDP has been observed to result in a reduction in the high friction properties of ZDDP, while simultaneously enhancing the wear resistance of the [NCDs][DEHP] under conditions of high load. This indicates that the combined additives exert a mutually reinforcing effect.
Prior to conducting the SEM and EDS analyses, the samples were subjected to cleaning with n-heptane, with the objective of removing residual lubricant on the surface.

The results of SEM maps and EDS elemental compositions of the abrasion area from the lower steel block are presented in Fig. 5. In comparison to the PAO10 lubrication, the width and depth of the wear scars corresponding to the lubrication with ZDDP, [NCDs][DEHP], and ZDDP + [NCDs][DEHP] are significantly decreased, and the width and depth of the wear scars for the ZDDP + [NCDs][DEHP] are the minimum. When PAO10 is utilized as the sole lubricant, a textured surface with numerous grooves is observed (Fig. 5(a), R a = 0.411 µm). In contrast, PAO10 + ZDDP results in a reduced width and depth of wear scars compared to PAO10, yet the surface roughness increases to R a = 0.456 µm (Fig. 5(b)). This suggests that ZDDP can mitigate wear by forming a protective film on the wear surface. However, the resulting roughened surface increases the friction coefficient and exhibits high friction.

The application of lubrication with [NCDs][DEHP] (PAO10 + [NCDs][DEHP] in Fig. 5(c), PAO10 + ZDDP + [NCDs][DEHP] in Fig. 5(d)) results in the formation of smoother wear scars, with PAO10 + [NCDs][DEHP] having the smoothest wear scars (R a = 0.165 µm). The surface roughness of wear scars for combined additives (R a = 0.213 µm) is intermediate between those lubricated by ZDDP and [NCDs][DEHP] alone. Therefore, when [NCDs][DEHP] is used in combination with ZDDP, the rough surface of ZDDP gives smoother and the high friction of ZDDP decreases. The morphology of wear marks lubricated with different lubricants is related to the substances they generate on the wear surface, and the differences in the compositions of tribofilm also affect the friction coefficient.

The results of the EDS indicate that the wear surfaces formed by the additives containing [NCDs][DEHP] exhibit reduced Fe signal, as illustrated in Fig. 5(c) and (d). This suggests that the protective films formed by [NCDs][DEHP] and ZDDP + [NCDs][DEHP], respectively, are thicker than those produced by ZDDP alone. The mixed protective film formed by the combination of ZDDP and [NCDs][DEHP] exhibits an enrichment of the C, indicating a carbon-containing protective film may be formed on the worn surface, in which [NCDs][DEHP] could be the main source of C. In comparison to ZDDP, the content of Zn in the hybrid film increased (Fig. 5(d)), suggesting that Zn is derived exclusively from ZDDP. This implies that the mixed protective film contains a certain proportion of ZDDP-related substances.

It can be observed that the working mechanism of the ZDDP tribofilm differs from that of the [NCDs][DEHP] tribofilm. ZDDP functions as a barrier to anti-wear by forming tribofilm to protect the rubbing surface. However, this may result in elevated friction. The NCD cations and phosphate ester anions in [NCDs][DEHP] interaction on the metal surface, forming low shear layers of [NCDs][DEHP] exhibit low friction. The disparate working mechanisms results in discrepancies in friction coefficient and wear scars. The incorporation of [NCDs][DEHP] can render smoother rubbing surface.

The EDS results demonstrate that [NCDs][DEHP] is involved in the tribochemistry of ZDDP, and reveal a significant increase in the content of elemental C on the rubbing surface of the combined additives, accompanied by a decrease in the content of elemental Fe. To further determine the composition and chemical bonding state of the combined additives tribofilm, the wear scars of PAO10 + [NCDs][DEHP] and PAO10 + ZDDP + [NCDs][DEHP] are subjected to XPS analysis.

The C 1 s spectrum of Fig. 6(a) exhibits three distinct peaks, which attributes to the C−C/CC, C−O/C−N, and CO bonds, respectively. The respective binding energies of these peaks are 284.8 eV, 285.8 eV, and 288.04 eV. As illustrated in Fig. 6(b), the Fe component of the tribo-pair underwent oxidation to form Fe₃O₄ and Fe₂O₃ within the sliding contact. The peak observed at 719.4 eV is satellite peak [33]. The FeS signal is observed at 713.6 eV on the wear surface of PAO10 + ZDDP + [NCDs][DEHP]. The binding energy signal of C−N at 399.9 eV in Fig. 6(c) is primarily attributed to the adsorption of NCD cations onto the metal surface. Additionally, a peak of organic nitride is observed at 397.9 eV [34] in the combined additives' N 1 s spectrum, indicating the involvement of [NCDs][DEHP] in the tribochemistry of ZDDP. The peak at 133.9 eV of the P 2p spectrum in Fig. 6(d) is attributed to either metal phosphates or P = O bonds. Fig. 6(e) and (f) demonstrate that the wear surfaces of POA10 + ZDDP + [NCDs][DEHP] contain metal phosphates (such as (Zn)3(PO4)2), sulfides (such as FeS and ZnS) and sulfates, which are derived from ZDDP. It is noteworthy that in Fig. 6(f), sulfides are the predominant chemical state of sulfur in PAO10 ZDDP+ [NCDs][DEHP] tribofilm, whereas sulfates are the products formed by the intense shear oxidation of sulfides.

In conclusion, the energy spectrum of [NCDs][DEHP] demonstrates that NCD cations and phosphate ester anions adsorb on the friction contact surfaces, which are involved in the construction of tribofilms through tribochemical reactions. When [NCDs][DEHP] and ZDDP are used as additives, resulting in the formation of hybrid tribofilms comprising a range of organic nitrogen compounds, as well as FeS, (Zn)3(PO4)2, FeSO4, FePO4, ZnS, Fe4O3, and Fe2O3, among other substances.

XPS spectrum indicates the presence of carbon signals in the combined additives. To determine the origin of elemental C in the hybrid tribofilms, the worn surfaces are analyzed by Raman spectrum, with measurement range of 200–2000 cm−1. The measuring results presented in Fig. 7 demonstrate the presence of characteristic peaks associate with Fe2O3 at 224, 292 and 409 cm−1 in the worn surfaces of PAO10 and PAO10 + [NCDs][DEHP] samples. The peak at 665 cm−1 is attributed to Fe3O4 [35,36] . However, on the wear surfaces lubricated with ZDDP and the combined additives, the peak intensity of the Fe2O3 and Fe3O4 characteristic peaks is relatively weak or even insignificant, indicating that the presence of ZDDP inhibits the generation of iron oxides.

In Fig. 7(a) (70 N), the D and G peaks are detected at approximately 1350 and 1590 cm−1 [37] for [NCDs][DEHP] and ZDDP + [NCDs][DEHP], indicating that the lubricant containing [NCDs][DEHP] produces carbon tribofilm in sliding contact. It is noteworthy that the D and G peaks of C signals are not exhibit substantial intensity in ZDDP + [NCDs][DEHP], indicating that a thicker carbon tribofilm does not exist in the hybrid tribofilm. The construction of the [NCDs][DEHP] carbon film in the combined additives results in reduction of high friction observed with ZDDP. This is duo to the self-mending ability of the carbon film, which enable the roughened surface of the ZDDP tribofilm to be improved. As a result, the hybrid tribofilm formed by the combination of ZDDP and [NCDs][DEHP] is able to provide excellent lubrication performance.

Fig. 7(b) depicts the Raman analysis of the worn surface at 140 N. In comparison to Fig. 7(a), the analysis results of [NCDs][DEHP] at 140 N exhibit a notable absence of discernible D and G peak signals. This observation suggests that the carbon tribofilm constructed by [NCDs][DEHP] is susceptible to damage under high load, indicating a state of dynamic equilibrium between its formation and destruction. This provides an explanation as to why the lubrication performance is not optimal at high load. Indeed, the presence of ZDDP enables the hybrid tribofilm generated by the combined additives to remain durably and unbrokenly at high load. This suggests that ZDDP enhances the extreme pressure performance of the combined additives and the tribofilm generated by ZDDP can also facilitate the formation of carbon tribofilm in the presence of NCD cations (see Fig. 7(b), PAO10 + ZDDP + [NCDs][DEHP]).

To elucidate further insight into the lubrication mechanism of the combined additives, the optical images of morphological features for the tribofilm on the abraded surface in the presence of ZDDP, [NCDs][DEHP] and ZDDP + [NCDs][DEHP] are presented in Fig. 8(a-c). The ZDDP tribofilm, as observed in Fig. 8(a), is distinguished by the characteristic of thin blue streaks, indicative of phosphates, and brown clusters, suggestive of sulfides, like structures [38,39] . The darker in color of the [NCDs][DEHP] worn surface in Fig. 8(b), when considered alongside the results of XPS and Raman analyses, suggests the production of a carbon-based tribofilm and iron oxides on the rubbing surface. Fig. 8(c) illustrates that in the case of the combination of ZDDP and [NCDs][DEHP], the hybrid tribofilm exhibits a combined characteristic of the tribofilms produced by the two additives alone. This is manifested as a black carbon film covering on the dense blue patches.

In order to ascertain the elemental distribution of the hybrid tribofilm, Fig. 8 (d) depicts the outcomes of XPS deep elemental sputtering, wherein the data substantiate the presence of a considerable quantity of elemental C on the surface of the hybrid tribofilm. This finding is in accordance with the appearance of the D and G peaks in the Raman results of combined additives in Fig. 7, thereby suggesting that the carbon film is present and predominantly situated on the surface of the hybrid tribofilm. Upon sputtering time reaching the 6 min, the Fe signal begins to intensify, accompanied by a rise in the levels of Zn, P and S. This indicates the emergence of the distinctive elemental distribution associated with the ZDDP tribofilm. Upon the ion beam reaching the steel surface, Zn, P and S signals decrease, while the N signal remains present but at a lower level. The concentration of phosphates and sulfides relative elements demonstrates an initial increase, followed by a subsequent decline with the prolongation of sputtering time, indicating that they are more prevalent in the lower portion of the hybrid tribofilm.

To ascertain the lubrication regime of the tribo-pair lubricated with ZDDP, [NCDs][DEHP] and ZDDP + [NCDs][DEHP], the minimum lubricating film thickness (h min) is estimated by utilizing the Hamrock-Dowson formula [40].
(1)
h
min
= 2.69
G
0.53
U
0.67
R
W
0.067
( 1 − 0.61
e
- 0.73 c
)
where, G = αE', U = ηu / E′R, W = w / E′R 2, the pressure viscosity coefficients (α) of ZDDP, [NCDs][DEHP] and ZDDP + [NCDs][DEHP] are employed 22 GPa−1 [41], 24 GPa−1 [42], 24.5 GPa−1, respectively. E′ is the equivalent elastic modulus, R is the effective radius of the wear scar for steel ball [43]. η is the dynamic viscosity equal to the product of kinematic viscosity and density at room temperature in Table 1; c is a coefficient, generally taken as 1.

As indicated by Eq. (1), the minimum lubricating film thicknesses calculated following the addition of [NCDs][DEHP], ZDDP, and ZDDP + [NCDs][DEHP] are 34.42 nm, 36.02 nm, and 36.53 nm, respectively, at 70 N and 3 Hz (relative sliding velocity of approximately 24 mm/s).

The film thickness ratio (λ) [44] to ascertain the lubrication regime:
(2)
λ =
h min
σ 1
2
+
σ 2
2
Where, σ 1 and σ 2 denote the root mean square surface roughness of the steel ball and block, respectively. (about 1.2 times the mean surface roughness). The values of λ for [NCDs][DEHP], ZDDP and ZDDP + [NCDs][DEHP] are 1.307, 1.368 and 1.387, respectively. These values indicate that the tribo-pair is in the mixed lubrication regime.

Fig. 9 illustrates a schematic diagram of the lubrication mechanism of ZDDP in conjunction with [NCDs][DEHP] as additive. Upon [NCDs][DEHP] with good ionic structure entering the contact zone during the sliding process, the phosphate ester anions adsorb onto the metal rubbing surface by electrostatic attraction, resulting in the generation of phosphate compounds through tribochemical reaction. Concurrently, NCD cations can also adsorb on the rubbing surface, facilitated by the presence of phosphate ester anions. When the two additives coexist, the polar ends of ZDDP molecules to facilitate the rapid adsorption and reaction, which results in the formation of tribofilm comprising amorphous polyphosphates (Fig. 8(a)). The presence of the phosphate layer is also beneficial in promoting the rapid and effective adsorption of NCD cations to the metal surface, thereby contributing to the formation of the hybrid tribofilm. During the friction process, the mending effect of NCDs mitigated the elevated friction resulting from surface roughening of the ZDDP tribofilm under mixed lubrication regime. The interaction between ZDDP and [NCDs][DEHP] through the tribochemical reaction, ultimately resulted in the formation of a phosphate hybrid tribofilm with carbon-rich on the surface (Fig. 8(c)).

Furthermore, the complementary effect of the combined additives is demonstrated in that, on the one hand, [NCDs][DEHP] can exert its self-mending property to reduce the high friction of ZDDP under mixed lubrication regime, thereby enhancing the lubrication performance of the combined additives. On the other hand, according to the friction experiments as well as the results of the Raman analysis (Fig. 7(b)), the carbon film generated by [NCDs][DEHP] lacks sufficient strength and is susceptible to damage under high load, leading to an increase in wear, while the incorporation of ZDDP has been shown to enhance the load-bearing capacity of the hybrid tribofilm. Therefore, the synergistic strategy of [NCDs][DEHP] and ZDDP effectively enhance the anti-friction and wear resistance properties of the lubrication system.

The effectiveness of ionic NCDs and ZDDP as combined additive in solid-solid contact was investigated. and it was found that their lubricating properties were enhanced by the formation of a phosphate hybrid tribofilm with carbon-rich on the surface through tribochemistry reaction. The lubrication mechanism underlying the synergistic strategy of ionic NCDs and ZDDP was elucidated through a combination of wear surface analysis and elemental depth sputtering. The principal conclusions are as follows:
(1)
When used in conjunction with ZDDP, the addition of a low quantity of [NCDs][DEHP] augments the anti-friction and anti-wear characteristics of ZDDP.
(2)
[NCDs][DEHP] mend the surface roughened tribofilm of ZDDP under mixed lubrication regime, resulting in a reduction in the high friction coefficient of ZDDP.
(3)
The carbon tribofilm generated by the tribochemical reaction between NCD cations and phosphate ester anions is insufficient strength to withstand high load, resulting in increased wear. In contrast, the addition of ZDDP enhances the load-bearing strength of the hybrid tribofilm.
(4)
During the friction process, more NCD cations are induced to participate in the friction interactions by the promotion of phosphate ester anions and ZDDP. NCDs as C source together with Zn, P, and S in ZDDP constructs tribochemical reaction film with rich carbon on the surface. This effectively inhibits direct contact of tribo-pair and enhances the anti-friction and wear-resistant performance of the lubrication system.

Feng Guo: Visualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Haocheng Wang: Resources, Conceptualization. Jiali Chen: Investigation. Xiaohu Pei: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Xiaoling Liu: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization.

If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
